concept_ref: §3 row 3 — 'Decision coverage = Branch Condition coverage' (corrected by §5.3.4)

scenario.title: Two-Factor Login
scenario.narrative: A login function admits a user only when they are logged in AND have permission. Pick the test set that satisfies BOTH 100% Decision coverage (overall outcome flips T and F) AND 100% Branch Condition coverage (each individual condition observed T and F).

claim: Which candidate test set satisfies BOTH Decision coverage AND Branch Condition coverage for `is_logged_in AND has_permission`?
charges: The defendant function show_page(is_logged_in, has_permission) admits a user only when both conditions are true and shows an error otherwise.
The prosecution alleges that several candidate test sets falsely claim Decision-and-Branch-Condition coverage while leaving one or both criteria unmet.
The court must identify which candidate set genuinely satisfies both Decision coverage and Branch Condition coverage for the predicate `is_logged_in && has_permission`.

options.label: Set A — { (T,F) → error, (F,T) → error }
options.explanation: Decision is NOT satisfied — both tests produce the FALSE outcome, so the if-statement never evaluates to TRUE. Branch Condition for is_logged_in and has_permission individually is fine, but Decision coverage requires both T and F outcomes overall.

options.label: Set B — { (T,T) → show, (T,F) → error, (F,T) → error }
options.explanation: Correct. (T,T) gives the TRUE outcome, (T,F) and (F,T) give FALSE outcomes — Decision is covered. is_logged_in is observed T (in T,T and T,F) and F (in F,T); has_permission is observed T (in T,T and F,T) and F (in T,F). Both Decision AND Branch Condition coverage are satisfied.

options.label: Set C — { (T,T) → show }
options.explanation: Only the TRUE outcome of the decision is exercised. The FALSE branch (error path) is never reached, so Decision coverage is 50%, not 100%.

options.label: Set D — { (T,T) → show, (F,T) → error }
options.explanation: Decision is satisfied (T then F outcome), and is_logged_in flips T→F. But has_permission is stuck at TRUE in both tests — Branch Condition for has_permission is NOT satisfied (no F observation).

correct_answer_explanation: Decision coverage requires the overall if-statement to produce both T and F outcomes. Branch Condition is stricter: each condition must appear as T and F across the test set. Set B is the smallest set that satisfies both for `is_logged_in && has_permission`.
wrong_answer_explanation: Check Decision and Branch Condition independently. Decision needs at least one TRUE outcome and one FALSE outcome overall. Branch Condition needs each of is_logged_in and has_permission to be observed as TRUE and as FALSE.
hints: Decision testing asks: did the if-statement produce both TRUE and FALSE outcomes overall? Branch Condition asks something stricter — each individual condition must also appear as both T and F.

Look at is_logged_in and has_permission separately. Did each appear as both TRUE and FALSE across the tests? Are any of them stuck on a single value? And does the decision overall produce both T and F?
Set B uses three tests. (T,T) gives T outcome; (T,F) and (F,T) give F outcomes (Decision ✓); is_logged_in and has_permission each appear T and F (BC ✓).

seeded_faults.description: Permission check is silently skipped — a user with is_logged_in=True is granted access regardless of has_permission.

misconceptions.explanation_md: Decision coverage tests only the overall outcome of the predicate (T/F at the if-statement). Branch Condition coverage requires *each individual condition* to take both TRUE and FALSE across the test set. A test set whose outcomes are all FALSE may exercise both conditions as T/F individually yet still fail Decision coverage; a test set that pins one of the two conditions to a single value satisfies Decision but fails Branch Condition.
